metadata.seed: Yeah. I was at a party, and I was kind of hesitant about going, and then I went and I realized, like, I just can't talk. Like, I don't like meeting new people. I never know what to say. And then it's, like, an awkward silence, and then I just feel like I don't want to be there anymore.

patient.query: Yeah. I was at a party, and I was kind of hesitant about going, and then I went and I realized, like, I just can't talk. Like, I don't like meeting new people. I never know what to say. And then it's, like, an awkward silence, and then I just feel like I don't want to be there anymore.

automatic_thoughts: I never know what to say
I don't want to be there anymore

entailment: Fear of being in a small group (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you felt stuck and unsure of how to connect with others. What evidence do you have that you can't talk or that you always feel awkward? Can you think of a time when you did manage to engage in conversation, even briefly?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I do feel stuck sometimes. It’s like, even when I want to connect, I just freeze up or say the wrong thing. I remember a time at a friend's party where I did manage to chat with someone for a little while, but afterward, I just felt so anxious about how I came across. It’s frustrating because I want to be more comfortable.

automatic_thoughts: I feel anxious about how I came across

neutral: Fear of speaking on the phone (finding)
Fear of being in a small group (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing a pattern where you feel stuck and anxious after trying to connect. What moments stand out where you felt you did manage to chat, and how did you feel in those moments? Are there other ways you might interpret what happened at the party that could explain the anxiety you felt afterward?

patient.query: Yeah, there was this moment when I was talking about a show I liked, and the other person seemed interested. For a second, it felt okay, like maybe I wasn’t messing everything up. But afterward, I just started replaying the conversation in my head, worrying about what I said and if I came off as weird or boring. It’s like I can’t hold onto the good parts, just the anxiety.

automatic_thoughts: if I came off as weird or boring
behaviors: replaying the conversation in my head

entailment: Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)
neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of speaking on the phone (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling a lot of anxiety after the conversation. What evidence do you have that you came off as weird or boring? Are there any moments that suggest you might have been engaging or interesting instead?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: fear of saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: entailment

patient.query: I mean, I guess I don't have any clear evidence that I was boring, but it just feels that way in my head. There were a few laughs, and I think they were nodding along, but then I just spiral into thinking maybe they were just being polite. It's like I can’t trust those small moments. I just end up feeling like I’m always missing something.

automatic_thoughts: I can't trust those small moments

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried that people might not have enjoyed your company. What clues do you have that they were simply polite rather than genuinely engaged? Can you think of any moments that might suggest they were actually enjoying the conversation?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: automatic thoughts about others being polite
retrieved_concepts_used.label: unknown

patient.query: Yeah, I guess. They did laugh a couple of times, and we talked for a while, but then my brain just twists it around. I mean, maybe they were just being nice. It’s hard to hold onto those little bits of positivity when my mind starts jumping to the worst-case scenario. I want to believe I can be interesting, but it just feels like a rollercoaster of doubt.

automatic_thoughts: maybe they were just being nice

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing how quickly your thoughts can swing. What evidence do you have that the laughter was just friendly? Can you think of moments when you felt interesting and how that felt?

patient.query: Yeah, it’s like those swings happen so fast I hardly notice until I’m already worrying. I can think of a couple of moments where I felt really engaged in a conversation, like when I was talking about that trip I took last summer. I remember feeling animated and even excited, and there were smiles all around. But then it’s like that moment fades, and I’m back to feeling awkward and unsure. It’s discouraging.

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how quickly the excitement can shift into that uneasy feeling of awkwardness and uncertainty. What do you think might be happening in those moments when the high fades? Could there be other ways to view that change that might feel less discouraging?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: anxiety about saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

patient.query: It’s tough to say. I guess maybe I just get scared that I won’t be able to keep it going, you know? Like, what if I run out of things to say or say something silly? I mean, I know everyone has those moments, but for me, it feels like I'm on this tightrope, and one misstep will make it all crash down. I want to hold onto the good feelings longer, but that sudden switch just pulls me back into worrying.

triggers: fear of running out of things to say
automatic_thoughts: I won't be able to keep it going
one misstep will make it all crash down
I want to hold onto the good feelings longer

entailment: Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)
neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the fear of losing the flow feels like a tightrope walk. When you think about moments where you’ve managed to keep the conversation going, what helped you feel more secure? How might you view a small slip as a chance to learn rather than a crash?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: fear of saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: entailment

patient.query: I guess when I think back, it’s usually when I felt relaxed or maybe when the other person showed genuine interest. It made it easier to just go with the flow. But the idea of seeing a slip as a learning moment feels kind of foreign. It’s hard to imagine not feeling ashamed or embarrassed afterward. I don’t know, it just seems easier to focus on the mess-ups than to see them as part of the process. It’s like I’m always waiting for the next thing to

automatic_thoughts: seeing a slip as a learning moment feels foreign
easier to focus on the mess-ups

contradiction: Able to remember new motor skills (finding)
Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of shame whenever a slip happens. What evidence do you have that this shame is the only possible reaction? Have there been moments when a mistake actually helped you learn or grow, even if it felt uncomfortable at first?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I do feel a lot of shame. It’s like it sneaks in and takes over, and I can’t shake that feeling. I can remember times when I’ve messed up and, in hindsight, they taught me something or at least made me laugh later. But in the moment, it just feels overwhelming, like I’m trapped in that feeling of “I can’t believe I said that.” It’s frustrating because I know there could be a different way to look at it

automatic_thoughts: I can’t believe I said that

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how heavy that shame feels and how it can feel like a trap. What evidence do you have that this feeling of “I can’t believe I said that” is true, and what evidence might suggest a different view? How might you describe the situation to a close friend who wants to help you see it more gently?

patient.query: It’s really hard to think about it that way. I mean, I guess I feel like all the times I've stumbled over my words or said something awkward just weigh more than the times that went okay. But if I were talking to a friend, I might say something like, “Yeah, I tripped up a bit, but the other person laughed, and we were having a good chat.” It feels like there could be a gentler way to see those moments, but I just get stuck

automatic_thoughts: My awkward moments weigh more than the good ones
I can't see those moments in a gentler way

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response:
**
How do you know that the awkward moments weigh more than the good ones? What would you say to yourself if you were the friend you mentioned? Can you think of a time when a small stumble didn’t change how the conversation felt overall?
